title: Capsella turoniensis
taxonomy: Capsella turoniensis belongs to the family Brassicaceae. It is a member of the genus Capsella, which is a group of small, annual plants commonly known as shepherd's cress. The species is closely related to the more well-known shepherd's purse, Capsella bursa-pastoris. Capsella turoniensis is a relatively rare and endemic species found in certain regions. This plant is also referred to as the Turonian shepherd's cress due to its limited geographic range.
uses: Capsella turoniensis is an extinct species of flowering plant, and therefore, there is limited information available on its potential medicinal uses. However, its extinct status makes it challenging to explore any potential applications in modern medicine. As research on this species is scarce and it is no longer present in the wild, any possible medicinal uses remain speculative. Further research is necessary to uncover any potential benefits of Capsella turoniensis, even though it is no longer available for study.

preparations: Capsella turoniensis, a plant species found in the Mediterranean region, has not been extensively studied in terms of herbal preparations. However, its close relative, shepherd's purse (Capsella bursa-pastoris), is used in herbal medicine to treat various conditions, including colds, sore throats, and diarrhea. The leaves and stems of shepherd's purse are typically used to make infusions, which are consumed as teas. In some traditional medicine practices, shepherd's purse is also used to stop bleeding and as a laxative. Further research is needed to determine the potential uses of Capsella turoniensis in herbal preparations.
